speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two, interview with Mr. Zoltan Wieder. You were describing Shabbat in your house, what your mother cooked for Shabbat. What happened on Friday nights for Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday night, we went to-- to the synagogue, to shul, and my father, and myself, and my brother. And my mother lit the candles. She lit eight candles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why eight?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lit four children, plus there were two, six. And I don't know. She lit two more candles. I don't Know what purpose was it. But she lit two more candles. And we didn't have a special dining room. But my brothers in my room, which was-- we called it the white room, because he had white furniture in it. There, we had the table, the bigger table. And we were sitting at the table, a white tablecloth. And the challah was there, which my mother baked. And so he came home from the synagogue. We sang [HEBREW]. And then my father made kiddish. And we did-- both my brother and myself, we did too. And then we sit down. We washed. And we sit down to eat. And we had always had to gefilte fish. Sometimes, we had regular fish also. But it was hard to get it all the time. So she made that, like I said, that [INAUDIBLE] fish. And then we had chicken soup. We had meat. And we sang [HEBREW]. We talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any [HEBREW]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sing any for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I didn't forget it. And I know by heart, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just sing for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I could sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have guests come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after supper, they came, the relatives, over-- my uncle and my aunt, and they had two children. The girl was also called Blima, because they were all called after my grandmother. And we called her Helen Blima. Her mother still was Helen, Helen. My mother was [? Rali, ?] in Hungarian. And so my-- my sister was [? Rali ?] [NON-ENGLISH], they called, to distinguish between them. And my other cousin was Helen [NON-ENGLISH], after her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came over. And they had also a son. [? Itzu ?] was his name. And he was younger than me, two years younger-- actually not, I think-- because she was two years younger. He was four years younger than me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had another cousin. And they had father and mother. Her father was [NON-ENGLISH], my uncle Eugene. And Helen was the grandma. And until my grandfather left, we used to go to him Friday night until '36. I was a small child at that time. But after that, they came to us. And sometimes, I had another uncle, [INAUDIBLE]. [? Levy ?] was his name, [? Levy ?] [? Joseph. ?] And his wife was [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] her name. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It will come back to me, his wife's name. And that was-- they came-- and she had two children. They had two children, two girls, which I will talk about later. They were-- they escaped in [NON-ENGLISH]. One is Lily, who lives now. She is married and has a child in Schenectady. And the other one, the older one, was [? Blima ?] also. We called her [NON-ENGLISH] because she was bigger. [NON-ENGLISH] means big. And she was born in '22. She was-- I mean, she was-- I think in 22, right. So she was older than my sister. So we had three [? Blimas. ?] And actually, we had another [? Blima ?] in Czechoslovakia, which was [? Gunder ?] [? Blima, ?] because she had curly hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were all [? Blimas, ?] four. So they came also to us. Or sometimes we went to them. And yeah, that it was very lovely, the whole family there. Did this happen every Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost every Friday, we went to-- they came to us, or we went to them. And then Saturday afternoon, the same thing, they came, the relatives, and some others-- my mother had a lot of friends, and my father too. They came also, friends. And they met in the synagogue. And after the synagogue, Shabbas, we came home. We had Kiddush. And my mother had all kinds of cakes baked, [NON-ENGLISH], which is that-- they call it here the bow ties. And we called it [NON-ENGLISH]. And Danish, she did. She was a very good cook. I remember she did-- for Purim, when she did-- she was starting baking four weeks before Purim, because she sent out a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] to different-- to friends. And we were very happy at that time, because we went. We got a little either money or candies. And she baked all kind of torts, and all kind of stuff. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite holiday was Passover. My grandfather had a-- he had a winery in a town about 20 miles from Baia-Sprie. [NON-ENGLISH] they called it, [NON-ENGLISH] in Romanian. And they had there a winery. And we went to pick the-- to pick the grapes during the fall. And they had a press there. They made the wine there. And we loved the red wine that-- it wasn't fermented yet. It was sweet, like the grape juice here. But it was very good. And we loved that with my brother. But Passover months, especially, we drank all the-- you know, you have to drink the four cups. My brother and myself, we always drank the four cups complete. And we got a little dizzy Friday in the Seder nights. But we loved that holiday. We were so happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- what was your social life like as you were growing up? Who were your friends? Who did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we were growing up, we had-- we had the friends from my school, and the boys I grew up with. And one was like Mickey. The other one was [NON-ENGLISH]. The other one was Herschel. And we grew up together. And when we got a little older, 13, 14, usually Saturday we went there to the mountains, because this town was a town in the middle of a mountain. The mountains were around the town. And we went to the mountains. It was about two miles, three miles, four miles. It depends which place we went. We all went on a hike to the mountains. And then we played around. We played with the boys and girls Rummy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rummy meaning cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was especially, Monopoly, and other place. And we were very busy with studying, all of us, because we didn't have too much time, free time. And especially when I was already-- after the Hungarian came in, they didn't allow me to go to the high school, because it was [NON-ENGLISH]. They didn't allow Jews to the high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- my mother wanted very much we should go and graduate. So we start-- we went to college [INAUDIBLE]. There was-- this was [INAUDIBLE] was a Jewish gymnasium, officially recognized Jewish gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we learned at home in private. And we went to do the exams there once a month, or every three weeks or so, because we couldn't afford to go there, and sleep over there, and get a room, and bed, and food, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It also about 200 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back for one minute, you mentioned that your father often worked in the forest about 20 miles. How did he commute-- travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he traveled-- he had to hire a wagon with a horse. And he didn't go every day. He went twice a week or so, maybe three times a week, maybe once a week, depends. And he had to go. He had to pay for that. And he had to go. And I remember, I was a child. And I was always a worrier. I was worrying for everybody. And my daughter is the same. And in the evening at 6, 7, it got dark. And he wasn't home yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was worrying, all the time. What's happened? Maybe happened something to him. Sometimes, she got home at 8:00 o'clock at night, or 9:00 o'clock, always worrying. Were there cars already?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were-- they were here and there cars, but not people. People didn't have cars. Private people didn't have cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Buses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buses, there were some buses, but not-- when he went to the forest, they didn't go buses there. There were some buses between-- like there was a bus between [NON-ENGLISH], even it was only nine kilometers. But at that time, we considered this a big distance, you know? And sometimes, you went by bike to that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get back to what we were talking about, your relationship with your friends, were you-- were there any political groups in-- for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not [INAUDIBLE] political groups. We had-- actually, there was a Zionist movement. And there in our town was the [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a religious Zionist movement. And we activated there. We were part of them. We had meetings, get-togethers, on the Shabbat afternoon, or sometimes during the evening, some big days. And we talked about Israel at that time. And we sang Zionist songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what? What kind of Zionist songs were you singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, at that time-- like [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] what you sing today, the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't recall it now. But there were some Zionist songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Girls and boys together in the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, girls and boys together. And we learned some Hebrew, Hebrew language. And actually, I was tutoring some Hebrew, and some other young people in between '40 and '44. I made a little money with tutoring, because we need it for the school. So-- and there was-- there was some activity, Zionist activity there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books were you reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- I loved to read books. And even when I was a small child, my sister and me, we loved to read. We were reading all kind of story books in the beginning when we were children, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian mostly, and Romanian too. And later on, we were reading also classical books, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [? Zoller, ?] and Victor Hugo, and [? Cronin, ?] some of these classical books. That was later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have newspapers coming into the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had newspapers coming in. It was a local from-- the local paper was from [NON-ENGLISH], they called it. [NON-ENGLISH] was a river in [NON-ENGLISH], which-- [ROMANIAN] was in Romanian, and Hungarian was [HUNGARIAN], the name. And that river went flow [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] into-- it went to [NON-ENGLISH] too. And it went into the-- to another river, to the [NON-ENGLISH], which was in [NON-ENGLISH]. There are different rivers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the newspaper was called [HUNGARIAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [HUNGARIAN], yeah that newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Hungarian. And then we have a newspaper which come to us, there was [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a Jewish newspaper from [NON-ENGLISH], from [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], new, [NON-ENGLISH] means east, new east. And that was-- that paper is still in function in Israel, in Hungarian language, in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it originally came out in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was in Hungarian. It never was in Yiddish. It was in Hungarian. And we didn't have, actually, Yiddish papers there in Yiddish, actually. We didn't have it. There were some Yiddish papers in Romania. And inside Romania, they called it [NON-ENGLISH], in Bucharest or in [NON-ENGLISH], but not in-- in Transylvania, actually, we didn't have any Yiddish. We had it in Hungarian or Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the radio-- we got a radio in the house in 1940. I remember we got that radio from my uncle, who-- who got the new radio. He gave it to us. And we had on it only one station, that when the Hungarian [INAUDIBLE] Budapest [INAUDIBLE]. It was-- it was a radio-- you could listen to Budapest. And until 1940, we didn't have radio. That was a big deal, a radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on in Germany, of Hitler, of the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was aware what going on. And we didn't know what's happened actually, there. But we know there were a lot of anti-Semitic things going on in Germany, and Jews. But we didn't know what-- we didn't know they took them to concentration camps and these kind of things. We didn't know. And nobody said nothing about that. The papers didn't write about it. And later on-- and when we had the radio already, I couldn't listen to our radio, I went to my uncle, who had a better radio. And I could listen to the BBC News. We close all the windows, and all the doors, you know, because if they would have find you, you'd have been trouble. It take you to jail. And I was listening to the BBC. I was a child, I remember. And I was 13, 14 years old. And I always liked politics. And I was listening via news about, you know, about-- you know, because the papers there, what they were written, they were written only the victories, and so on, and whatever, about Hitler. And they were the Hungarian papers at that time. But I was listening to the radio. And I find-- but the BBC never said nothing about what's happening with the Jews, that they are killing Jews or something. We never knew anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know when the war broke out in 19-- what did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the war broke out in 1939, September. I did know, because I read the newspapers. I remember when Hitler attacked Poland. I knew about the [NON-ENGLISH]. I knew about the Vienna pact. And that was a tragedy, the Vienna pact, because they gave back part of Transylvania to Hungary, because the Romanian didn't give out the Jews. I had uncles and cousins which were in the southern part of Transylvania, which remained Romanian at that time. And they all escaped. And we were in the northern part of Transylvania, which became Hungary. And we were taken to concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any Polish Jews coming to your town, to Baia-Sprie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had-- we had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] polish family there. Actually, they were not-- but they were born in Poland. And my father worked for them, actually, in the forest. He had forest business. Wolf was his name. And because he was Polish origin, they took him away in 1942. And nothing--

speaker: interviewer
text: But nobody came-- refugees from Poland did not come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not refugees from Poland to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any attempts to leave? Did your family ever talk about leaving Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, my parents, especially my mother, because they grew up in Hungary, and they made their schools in Hungary, they were so happy when, in 1940, especially my mother, it became Hungary, because she remembered Hungarians before in 1918 when the Jews were free. There was no anti-Semitism there. And even it was all the time, but it wasn't freely or so. And she was actually happy at that time. I remember, she says, oh, how nice, Hungarian. We know better the language. They know-- we-- you know, they spoke Romanian too, but not that well that we spoke it, because we did our schools in Romanian. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I remember we never talked about it. First, we didn't know what to do, how to get out from there, because we didn't have the means, or something to-- and we always talked about, oh, look, what's going to happen with everybody will happen with us too. But it was such a-- we didn't imagine something like this can happen, because I know a few families, not from my town they escaped. They were hiding in-- because-- in the forest or someplace. We couldn't maybe hide in the forest and escape, because we needed only three, four months until the Russian came into Romania-- four, five months actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I meant prior to that, was there ever talk of immigrating?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was never talk about it there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody immigrate, people leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they couldn't go to Palestine at that time. I know-- yes, I know one family. They were my good friends. I was talking about that family who they took them away in '42. His-- he had a daughter and a son-in-law who had two children. And the son-in-law went to Palestine somewhere in '35 or '36, escaped some way. And the mother and the two children were in Romania. And they were very good friends of mine. We were approximately the same age. The girl was actually a little older. She was my age. And the brother was two years younger. And they got out from there. His father arranged from Palestine with a-- with a children's transport in '42. And they went to Palestine. And I met this boy in Israel, in Jerusalem-- not boy, a young man, he has children. He's two years older-- younger than me. [NON-ENGLISH], and his last I don't recall right now exactly. I met him in Jerusalem. I find out he lives in Jerusalem. And when I was there in 1991, I met him. He is a director of the museum in Jerusalem, and also director of the tax-- sales tax, something like this with the tax. And so I met him there. And we were very happy. We talked. We were playing together.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things begin to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things began to change-- so in 1940-- actually, 1939, I was-- in [NON-ENGLISH], I was there for one year in the [INAUDIBLE] there. I learned there at the [INAUDIBLE]. And then in 1940, when the Hungarian came in, so I couldn't go to the high school there. It was public.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to-- me and my sister, we went to-- we learned privately. We went to give the exams there. And then already in '42, started already a lot of laws. Anti-Semite laws came out. Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Against Jews. They can-- first, they can't go to school. They can't go to the university. They can't go to high schools. And at first, it was numerous clauses. They left-- they allowed 10%, or 8%. Then it came [INAUDIBLE] when they didn't allow nothing. It was zero. And then it came out-- every day, we read in the newspapers, all kind of laws against Jews from Hungary, from Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't-- first about schools, then about-- they couldn't have elected offices. They couldn't be in the government, or in the city offices. And-- and I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the parliamentarians, when they were speaking in the parliament in Budapest. And I always-- we read the newspapers. We saw, they said, we don't want the Jews here. We want to be between us. We don't need the Jews around us. We just want to be between us, and all kind of things like this. And then the businesses, they were restricted, the Jewish businesses. The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it started they give out flour and different food on tickets. And they put all this flour, all this food, at Hungarian stores. Jewish stores didn't get any allotments.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were all restricted in doing business, and getting jobs, government jobs or other jobs city jobs, whatever. And then it started-- we had a big snow there all the time. That part of Romania was-- we had snow from November to April.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started taking out the young people, and even older people, cleaning snow, shoveling snow, they just-- the Jews in '42, '43, and '44, beginning of '44, took out all the time to snow cleaning, snow shoveling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you ever taken to clean snow? d I wasn't taken there, because I was younger. And-- but I was-- I was shoveling a lot of snow there near my house. But my father was taken. And-- and other people were taken. And younger people were taken. Other than school, in what way did the new laws in '40 and '42 affect you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, personally, actually other than school, it affected-- you know, I saw this anti-Semite especially when they had the [HUNGARIAN] organization, the [HUNGARIAN] party. That was the Hungarian Fascist Party, [HUNGARIAN]. And they-- when we went to Shul, they run after us, try to hit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some got hit. Some-- some-- and there was calling all kind of names after us, you know? And then we were restricted in different things what we couldn't do. I'm going to change our tape again.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
